pages.text:
10 July 2025
ESMA42-2004696504-8164
Peer Review Report: Executive
Summary
Fast-track peer review on [a CASP] authorisation and supervision in Malta

Executive Summary
The Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA), that came into force on 29 June 2024, is
a major milestone in the EU’s approach to regulating digital assets. MiCA sets a new
comprehensive EU regulatory framework for crypto assets across the EU that aims to create
a unified legal framework that allows for consistency in the regulatory approach related to
Crypto Asset Service Providers (CASPs).
MiCA also introduces a common authorisation approach that all NCAs needs to follow. From
January 2025, CASPs must apply for an authorisation to operate within the EU. A
grandfathering period of up to 18 months (i.e. until 30 June 2026) allows existing crypto
assets providers, that provided their services in accordance with applicable law before 30
December 2024, to continue operating until authorised under MiCA. By applying the option
to shorten the transitional regime under MiCA, NCAs have ended-up with different
grandfathering periods creating different timelines as to when CASPs need to be authorised
in their respective jurisdictions.
MiCA is the first legislation in the world that sets a comprehensive regulatory framework in
the crypto space. This dynamic and rapidly evolving field requires consistent and robust
approaches across NCAs to ensure that a convergent approach applies from the outset.
Currently most NCAs are in the process of authorising CASPs. It is critical to foster trust in
this sector that bears high inherent risks. Therefore, the authorisation process should be
considered as the gatekeeper that enables NCAs to properly assess that CASP applications
are compliant with the regulatory requirements, including that key risks of the CASP
business model are effectively mitigated.
A specificity of this sector is that the most prevailing business model is a single entity hub in
Europe providing crypto services, through a passport, to the entire EU single market, while
the main client base is not necessarily in the home NCA. The single market is important to
broaden investors’ opportunities, and needs as a pre-requisite strong supervision of
products and services provided on a cross-border basis. This is another reason why CASP
authorisation is of paramount importance to ensure that investors in any jurisdiction across
the EU will be given the adequate level of protection, regardless of the jurisdiction of the
origin of the CASP offering these services.
Another ramification of this business model is the need to build trust among NCAs that the
actions and decisions taken by the home NCA foster a unified capital market that will
increase investor protection. NCAs will interchange the role of home and host NCA, so
ensuring a sound supervisory framework is beneficial to all. Therefore, all NCAs have a role
1

to play and can benefit from each other’s reinforced supervisory practices for the supervision
of the cross-border activities of CASPs.
In December 2024, the Board of Supervisors (BoS) agreed on the adoption of a coordinated
approach for CASPs authorisations, through (i) the adoption of a supervisory briefing on the
Authorisation of Crypto Asset Service Providers (CASPs) under MiCA and (ii) discussions
of key CASPs authorisation cases - prior to granting the authorisation - submitted to NCAs
in the dedicated ESMA Group, the Digital Finance Standing Committee (DFSC).
Following a series of events, the BoS of ESMA decided, in April 2025, to launch a peer
review on the authorisation and early supervision of a CASP by the Malta Financial Services
Authority (MFSA). Although the peer review targeted one NCA, still, it aims to foster
supervisory convergence and improve the supervisory practices of all NCAs, at a time when
consistency across NCAs in authorisation and setting supervisory expectations is key.
The peer review was carried out based on ESMA’s Peer Review Methodology (the
Methodology) by an ad hoc Peer Review Committee (PRC) following the approach foreseen
for fast-track peer reviews.
Assessment areas
The peer review covered:
(i) The MFSA’s authorisation process of the CASP entity and whether MFSA adequately
assessed that the CASP entity met the requirements for authorisation (including in terms of
shareholders structure and governance, group structure and reliance on intragroup entities
and services, including IT systems), notably with regards to risks from the functioning of the
[…] booking model, the adequacy of the use of Web3 products, potential conflicts of
interests, enforcement processes, the entity’s ability to comply with AML/CFT/Sanctions
requirements,
(ii) the supervisory review and use of adequate powers by MFSA on the CASP entity in
reaction to events that happened to the entity following its authorisation, including as to
whether it still met the conditions for its authorisation, and
(iii) the overall adequacy of the supervisory set-up and resources of the MFSA to carry-out
the above.
Through the assessment of documents submitted by the relevant Maltese authorities and
the on-site visit, the peer review assessed MFSA against the supervisory expectations in
these areas as set out in the peer review mandate.
2

Overall findings
The PRC observed that MFSA has built a good level of expertise in this sector and has
sufficient supervisory resources for CASP authorisations and supervision. However, this is
still an evolving landscape and MFSA needs to monitor closely the growth in authorisation
applications and supervised entities and identify in a timely manner any need to scale up or
adjust supervisory practices.
On the authorisation process of the CASP entity, the PRC questions the timing of the
authorisation of the specific entity under MiCA given that several material issues remained
unresolved at the time of the authorisation. It is unclear to the PRC, why MFSA did not
leverage on the authorisation process to ensure that the entity would remedy key
deficiencies before the authorisation would be effective. The PRC is of the view that the
overall authorisation process should have been more thorough and conducted on a
sufficient time to allow MFSA to properly assess compliance against the MiCA framework.
Any NCA should consider the supervisory history of an entity when assessing a request for
authorisation. The PRC acknowledges that no authorisation is completely free of risk and
some non-material risks or issues could be addressed through the on-going supervision. In
this case, the PRC is of the view that the supervisory history of the entity formed part of the
authorisation assessment, but it was not adequately considered. The PRC acknowledges
that MFSA actively engaged on multiple occasions and on several topics with the entity
under the previous national regime, which allowed MFSA to obtain good knowledge of the
entity’s functioning. However, material issues remained unresolved or pending remediation
at the time of the authorisation, including the remediation of previous enforcement cases
and the outcome of (at the time) pending ones.
The PRC did not find evidence that certain key aspects of the authorisation file were
adequately assessed including (i) aspects of its business plan related to its growth and the
on-boarding of new clients, (ii) potential conflicts of interest (iii) governance arrangements
(iv) risks related to the ICT infrastructure, custody, use of the […] booking model, use of
Web3 services and (v) certain AML/CFT risks and controls.
Finally, on the supervisory response of the MFSA following certain events, the MFSA’s
supervisory response appears largely appropriate and proportionate. However, the PRC
considers that some of the measures could have already been considered as part of the
authorisation process.
3

Overall assessment
The table below summarises the PRC’s assessment of the MFSA in each assessment area
based on the Methodology's benchmarks.
Supervisory settings and resources
The authorisation process
The supervisory review and use of adequate
powers
Fully meeting Largely meeting Partially meeting Not meeting
expectations expectations expectations expectations
Recommendations
There are specific recommendations in the report that are addressed to MFSA. These are
mainly related to the findings of the PRC relevant to the authorisation of the specific CASP.
The PRC recommends that the MFSA assesses material issues that either were still pending
at the date of the authorisation or that have not been adequately considered at the
authorisation stage.
However, several recommendations are relevant to all NCAs in their ongoing / upcoming
CASP authorisations. The PRC encourages NCAs to regularly share through the DFSC or
other relevant EU level Standing Committees the key risks and mitigations for key CASPs
under their supervision. NCAs are encouraged to make use of experiences and guidance
shared through the DFSC to inform their authorisation decisions thus fostering a convergent
approach. The PRC did not provide any recommendations on policy issues where
discussions are still on-going at the DFSC level such as the […] booking model and custody
but strongly encourages NCAs to continue such discussions.
Due to the novelty and nature of these type of entities as well as the inherent risks of their
business model, the PRC recommends to all NCAs, currently in the process of authorising
CASPs, to pay particular attention to certain aspects of the authorisation, related to:
4

(i) business growth where it is important to keep up with adequacy of resources, approaches
and systems. The PRC recommends to NCAs to assess entities’ business plans in a
forward-looking manner, notably to consider the expected business growth and the
underlying risks. The PRC encourages NCAs to make use of the DFSC to keep sharing
information on their supervisory settings, resources and tools for CASPs supervision, so to
help them identify the appropriate level of resources and build efficient supervisory models.
(ii) Conflicts of Interests including those related to the combination of CASPs services and
the related disclosure requirements,
(iii) governance and intragroup arrangements. Particularly, for the supervision of the
intragroup arrangements, the PRC encourages NCAs to use as baseline the cross-cutting
principles on third-party risks supervision that ESMA has recently published,
(iv) ICT architecture including intragroup reliance and use of sub-providers. Given it is
essential for a CASP’s business to have resilient IT systems, the PRC recommends that,
before granting an authorisation, NCAs review the ICT systems (including business
continuity) in light of DORA requirements. Higher scrutiny should be given to the functions
and services that are most critical to the CASP business (e.g. custody).
The PRC also highlights the high importance of ICT security for CASPs which encompasses
the capacity of the entity to appropriately and effectively react in case of a hack. The PRC
considers that NCAs should ensure, as part of the authorisation assessment, that the entity
has adequate measures in place to efficiently and effectively block malicious transactions if
needed.
(v) Web3 and decentralised products, including the promotion of unregulated services. The
PRC recommends that NCAs adequately assess the inherent exposure to DeFi risks and
that adequate controls are put in place to manage them. NCAs should also evaluate the
promotion of any unregulated services, including on whether the way they are presented to
customers might create confusion on these services appearing as being possibly regulated.
As gate keepers of the EU single market and given the hub business model in the crypto
space, the PRC recommends NCAs to further reflect on how EU investors can be equally
considered, wherever they are in the EU, and are able to apply their rights under this legal
framework. NCAs should also carefully consider the impact this may have on their own
resources and need to coordinate with other EU supervisors to consider their needs, views
and intelligence (e.g. complaints), as well as to adequately cooperate with them.
5

The PRC encourages NCAs to review, as part of the authorisation assessment, user
interfaces and customer journeys to ensure that relevant risk warnings are clearly presented
to users and that the overall customer experience is in line with MiCA requirements.
Finaly, this cross-border model requires NCAs to build high quality supervisory approaches
promoting effective cooperation and intensive convergence work.
Good practices
The PRC positively regards the pro-activeness of MFSA to recruit and build a pool of
supervisors with expertise on crypto services by (i) having targeted and active engagement
with local universities, (ii) providing high number of trainings and (iii) having active
cooperation with other relevant authorities. The PRC also positively regards the pro-active
outreach of the MFSA to the industry to encourage early preparation for MiCA transition and
to communicate expectations in a timely manner.
6

Annex - Statement from MFSA
MFSA Welcomes ESMA Peer View on CASP Authorisation and Supervision
The Malta Financial Services Authority (MFSA) welcomes the publication of the European
Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) Peer Review on CASP Authorisation and
Supervision and its overall findings that Malta is fully meeting expectations with regards to
supervisory settings and resources, largely meeting expectations with regards to supervisory
reviews and use of adequate powers in relation to the recently introduced Markets in Crypto-
Assets Regulation (MiCA) as well as partially meeting expectations in the authorisation
process.
The insights provided in the review are invaluable in supporting the MFSA’s continued efforts
to improve and strengthen its performance in the supervision of this sector, and the MFSA
commends the Peer Review Committee (PRC) (composed of staff from ESMA, European
Banking Authority (EBA) and National Competent Authorities (NCAs)) for their professional
and rigorous approach, executed under very tight timelines. Transparency and cooperation
are core to our philosophy, and we believe that by working closely with ESMA and our peers,
we can enhance convergence and strengthen our collective supervisory efforts.
The report affirms that the MFSA possesses the necessary resources, expertise, and
knowledge to carry out effective authorisation, supervision, and enforcement within this sector.
Malta established itself as an early regulatory adopter with respect to crypto assets back in
2018 when it introduced the Virtual Financial Assets (VFA) framework. MFSA is proud of its
track record during this time and the report highlights several good practices identified during
the review such as the MFSA’s active engagement with local universities and nurturing of
young professionals, the high number of trainings delivered to staff on key technical issues,
and the active cooperation between prudential and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) authorities,
as well as with various international supervisory, intelligence and law enforcement authorities
and counterparts.
While the report recognises MFSA’s capabilities, it also highlights opportunities where we can
further enhance our processes, as well as making recommendations for National Competent
Authorities (NCAs) across the EU. We view this as an opportunity to reinforce our processes
and procedures with respect to this innovative sector and the MFSA and FIAU are onboarding
the recommendations as provided by the review to ensure that Malta strengthens the
robustness of its supervisory framework in this field.
The MFSA respects the views of the PRC and accepts the findings of the report, while
recognising that NCAs across the EU are currently navigating effective implementation of the
MiCA regulations in their relevant jurisdictions.
7

We thank ESMA, the EBA and National Competent Authorities for their constructive
engagement and support during this process and look forward to continuing to work together
to improve and enhance authorisation and supervision processes for digital assets both in
Malta and across the EU.
8
